content.full_text:
.,. 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 .J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Scott E. Porter 
Counsel 
Jones and Mayer 
3777 Harbor Blvd. 
Fullerton, CA 92835 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-215 
Dear Mr. Porter: 
November I 0, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Greg Sebourn, Mayor of 
Fullerton, regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we arc only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
May the mayor make, participate in making or influence decisions related to Chevron's 
application to subdivide and then develop property in the city? 
CONCLUSION 
Because none of the mayor's interest will be foreseeably materially affected by the decision, 
the mayor would not have a conflict of interest. 
FACTS 
Mayor Sebourn is the Mayor of Fullerton, and one of five members of the City Council. He 
is employed by Stantec Consulting Services, Inc., a company with more than 15,000 employees 
worldwide that is listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Mayor Sebourn does not own any stock 
or stock options in the company. He does not sit on its board of directors or hold any official title 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Di\'ision 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-215 
Page No. 2 
within the corporation, although within his particular local office, he is known as a "lead" who 
oversees a small team of professionals on particular surveying projects. 
Chevron owns real property in Fullerton worth roughly $200 million. Chevron has applied 
to the City of Fullerton to subdivide and then develop the property with 760 residential units. The 
Planning Commission voted 6-1 to approve the project, and the City Council expects to conduct a 
public hearing on the matter due to an appeal. 
The mayor and his family have no financial interests in Chevron. He does not own any of its 
stock (except that which he may own via mutual funds). He does not own any property within the 
vicinity of the Chevron property. He has not received and does not expect to receive any income or 
gifts from Chevron, and does not work for Chevron in any capacity. The only connection he has 
with Chevron is that he is employed by Stantec, and one of Stantec's many clients is Chevron. 
Chevron has already hired all of the consultants that it will use on the project, and Stantec is 
not among them. There is the theoretical possibility that if Chevron's project is approved, and then 
Chevron fires one of its existing consultants, that Chevron could hire new consultants, and Stantec 
could be the consultants chosen to provide consulting services. 
To the best of the Mayor's knowledge, regardless of whether the City in fact approves or 
denies Chevron's proposed development of the property, Stantec will not receive any more or less 
business from Chevron than it would otherwise. There are no known "inside deals" or any other 
reason that Stantcc would see any more or additional consulting work from Chevron in any way 
relating to this·proposal. The amount of the mayor's compensation from Stantec is in no way 
correlated to whether the project is approved. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87 I 00 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a go,·ernmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.} Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 
and include the following: 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
Under Section 87103(c) and (d), the mayor has a potentially disqualifying interest in Stantec. 
Moreover, income of an official includes a pro rata share of the income of a business entity or trust 
in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or 
greater. (Section 82030(a).) The mayor has no ownership interest in Stantec, thus he has no interest 
in the sources of income to Stantec. 


File No. A-15-2 t 5 
Page No. 3 
Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard 
for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
Under your facts, Stantec is not explicitly involved in the decision. Thus, as applied to Stantec, 
Regulation 1870 I (b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial 
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's 
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
Materiality: Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides for a source of income that is a business 
entity, "the financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the 
price of the business entity's publicly traded stock . ... " 
Since Stantec has no business relationship to the property decisions before the city council, 
it is not foreseeable that the financial effect of the decisions would contribute to a change in the 
price of the business entity's publicly traded stock. Therefore the mayor would not have a conflict 
of interest. 
lfyou have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
W. Wallace 
istant General Counsel
